revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Shinto_shrine#Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]

===''Nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|left|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|''nagare-zukuri''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on the non-gabled side, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name, the most common among shrines all over the country. It has a {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side).
Sometimes the basic layout consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|moya}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof) is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> The ''honden'' varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and at Matsuo Taisha.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

===''Nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|left|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|''nagare-zukuri''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on the non-gabled side, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives the style its name, the most common among shrines all over the country. It has a {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side).
Sometimes the basic layout consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|moya}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof) is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> The ''honden'' varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and at Matsuo Taisha.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

[[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|left|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on the non-gabled side, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives the style its name, the most common among shrines all over the country. It has a {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side).
Sometimes the basic layout consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|moya}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof) is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> The ''honden'' varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. [[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|left|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on the non-gabled side, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives the style its name, the most common among shrines all over the country. It has a {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side).
Sometimes the basic layout consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|moya}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof) is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> The ''honden'' varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
While seemingly completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style, common around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. Both in fact possess characteristics that show they evolved from temporary shrines built for mountain worship.
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and at Matsuo Taisha.
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
While seemingly completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style, common around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. Both in fact possess characteristics that show they evolved from temporary shrines built for mountain worship.

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>

They for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that both Kasuga Taisha and the Nagare-zukuri [[Kamo Shrine]] used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that the shrine had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/> They also share a veranda covered by a roof in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and at Matsuo Taisha.

Sometimes the basic layout consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|moya}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof) is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> The ''[[honden]]'' (sactuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
==''Nagare-zukuri'' and ''kasuga-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']] While seemingly completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'', common around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
They for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that both Kasuga Taisha and the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that the shrine had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/> They also share a veranda covered by a roof in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and at Matsuo Taisha.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
[[ja:流造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

They for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that both Kasuga Taisha and the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that the shrine had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/> They also share a veranda covered by a roof in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

[[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. [[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. The fact that the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the so-called {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.

[[ja:流造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable|gabled]] roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. The fact that the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the so-called {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sactuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|left|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
[[ja:流造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. The fact that the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the so-called {{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sactuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]''<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref>, but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{DEFAULTSORT:Nagare-Zukuri}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]
[[ja:流造]] [[zh:流造]]

[[File:UjigamiJinja-M1395.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. The fact that the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the so-called {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sactuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|left|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

revision.text:
[[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|flowing style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造|flowing gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. The fact that the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the so-called {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sactuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double flow style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|left|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|left|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

[[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''nagare-zukuri'''|流造|streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|'''nagare hafu-zukuri'''|流破風造|streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造|double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|Ujigami Shrine in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at Matsuo Taisha in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].

The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.

The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].

This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
