===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[moya (architecture)|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Nagare-zukuri]] [[it:Nagare-zukuri]] [[ja:流造]] [[pl:Nagare-zukuri]] [[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]] [[sv:Nagare-zukuri]] [[zh:流造]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q210684]] Wikidata name: nagare-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q210684]] Wikidata name: nagare-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q210684]] Wikidata name: nagare-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] characterized by a very asymmetrical [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>

This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|[[:en:Omoya|moya]]}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''[[:en:Hisashi (architecture)|hisashi]]'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, [[:en:Uji|Uji]]'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q210684]] Wikidata name: nagare-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} characterized by a very asymmetrical {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|{{ill|Omoya|en|Omoya|WD=Q3326980|lt=moya}}}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''{{ill|Hisashi (architecture)|en|Hisashi (architecture)|WD=Q7884608|lt=hisashi}}'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>

===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} characterized by a very asymmetrical {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|{{ill|Omoya|en|Omoya|WD=Q3326980|lt=moya}}}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''{{ill|Hisashi (architecture)|en|Hisashi (architecture)|WD=Q7884608|lt=hisashi}}'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nagare-zukuri]][[fr:Nagare-zukuri]][[it:Nagare-zukuri]][[ja:流造]][[pl:Nagare-zukuri]][[ru:Нагарэ-дзукури]][[sv:Nagare-zukuri]][[zh:流造]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg|thumb|275 px|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in Uji, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}]] The {{nihongo|'''''nagare-zukuri'''''|流造||streamlined roof style}} or {{nihongo|''nagare hafu-zukuri''|流破風造||streamlined gabled style}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} characterized by a very asymmetrical {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|qid=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}d roof ({{nihongo|''kirizuma-yane''|切妻屋根}}) projecting outwards on one of the non-gabled sides, above the main entrance, to form a portico (see photo).<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> This is the feature which gives it its name. It is the most common style among shrines all over the country. That the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side) makes it belong to the {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|qid=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}} style.
==Design== Sometimes its basic layout, consisting of an elevated {{nihongo|core|母屋|{{ill|Omoya|en|Omoya|qid=Q3326980|lt=moya|1=Omoya}}}} partially surrounded by a veranda called ''{{ill|Hisashi (architecture)|en|Hisashi (architecture)|qid=Q7884608|lt=hisashi|1=Hisashi (architecture)}}'' (all under the same roof), is modified by the addition of a room in front of the entrance.<ref name="ko"/> A ''nagare-zukuri'' ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary) varies in roof ridge length from 1 to 11 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'',<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> but is never 6 or 8 ''ken''.<ref name="jana">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagarezukuri.htm Nagare-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The most common sizes are 1 and 3 ''ken''. The oldest shrine in Japan, {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|qid=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}}'s [[Ujigami Shrine]], has a ''honden'' of this type. Its external dimensions are 5x3 ''ken'', but internally it is composed of three {{nihongo|sanctuaries|内殿|naiden}} measuring 1 ''ken'' each.<ref name="jana"/>
==Variation== ===''Ryōnagare-zukuri''=== {{nihongo|''Ryōnagare-zukuri''|両流造||double streamlined roof style}} is an evolution of the ''nagare-zukuri'' in which the roof flows down to form a portico on both non-gabled sides.<ref name=" ko"/> Examples are the ''honden'' at [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and that at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.
===''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''=== [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''[[honden]]'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' units]]While superficially completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' style actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|qid=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
